description: Control / falsifier hypotheses. Their role is to arm the instrument: a control passing or failing is a statement about instrument health, not a statement about the theory.

hypotheses.plain_statement: Naive / wrong operator scaling of the gauge action should fail or visibly diverge under the reconstruction metric.

expected_signature.expected_value: drift well outside the reconstruction tolerance, or amplitude explosion at scaled beta
expected_signature.pass_rule: wrong scaling produces a clearly out-of-tolerance signature

hypotheses.why_this_matters: If wrong scaling does not fail, the reconstruction metric cannot distinguish correct from incorrect gauge action — meaning every other Program 1 result is unreliable.
failure_means: the instrument cannot distinguish correct gauge behavior from wrong scaling
every result that depends on the same metric becomes unreliable until this is fixed
failure_does_not_mean: Program 1 witnesses are wrong (only that they may not be reliable yet)
possible_alternative_hypotheses: the wrong-scaling control needs a more sensitive metric
the wrong-scaling magnitude was too small to be visible
the implementation has a bug that suppresses the expected divergence
intended_inference_if_passed: the reconstruction metric distinguishes the correct gauge action from a wrong scaling
the falsifier is armed in this window
this does not, by itself, support the base claim
disallowed_conclusions: this supports Program 1's positive witnesses directly
this proves NC2 in any formal sense

hypotheses.plain_statement: A counterfactual beta (off the critical line) should diverge from the true / reconstructed prime count under the same gauge scales.

expected_signature.expected_value: clear divergence of the counterfactual beta from the true count
expected_signature.pass_rule: counterfactual beta is rejected by the divergence test for every tested scale

hypotheses.why_this_matters: Without this control, beta-stability would be uninformative: any value of beta could appear to pass. The beta falsifier must be armed for VAL-1 to be epistemically meaningful.
failure_means: the beta falsifier is not armed
VAL-1 results lose epistemic weight until this control is fixed
the divergence metric or window may need revision
possible_alternative_hypotheses: counterfactual divergence is non-monotonic in beta
the divergence metric needs to be normalized differently
the counterfactual range was too close to 1/2 to separate
intended_inference_if_passed: the beta-divergence falsifier is armed for the tested counterfactual range
VAL-1 results are epistemically meaningful in this window
this is not theory support — it is instrument health
disallowed_conclusions: passing this control supports Program 1 directly
